Regional dynamics — geography, flows, freight¶

The NWE crack we decomposed in 01_Market_Overview.ipynb and the physical balance we scoped in 02_Fundamentals.ipynb both sit on top of flows. Crude arrives by tanker from WAF, ME, the Baltic. Diesel arrives by LR from USG, India, the Arab Gulf; leaves by MR to WAF and Latin America. Every flow crosses a chokepoint (Bosphorus, Suez, Hormuz, Bab-el-Mandeb) and pays a freight rate (Worldscale %) that moves with geopolitical risk — as the April 2026 Hormuz blockade reminds us.

Seven sections, in the order a physical desk reads them on a Monday morning: crude basin → Russia reroute → EU import mix → diesel-gasoline premium → global trade structure → freight regime → FX (as a diagnostic, not a signal).

In [1]:
import sys
sys.path.insert(0, '..')
from src import RegionalAnalysis
ra = RegionalAnalysis().load()

[Regional] Spot 1842 | FX 6978 | EU imports 1369 | RU exports 1308 | Cracks 1261 | OPEC exports crude/products 2590/2858 rows | Freight clean/dirty 75/126 rows

1. Crude basin — Brent vs WTI¶

Brent (global waterborne) minus WTI (US Cushing landlocked) captures how tightly linked the Atlantic basin is. The gap widens on Brent-specific supply shocks (Russia-invasion 2022, Hormuz 2026) and narrows on US-crude shocks (shale-growth 2014-2016). A trader reads it two ways: as a direct spread trade, and as the trigger level at which US-crude exports into Europe become economic and close the gap mechanically.

In [2]:
ra.chart_brent_wti_spread();

Brent-WTI mean : +4.02 USD/bbl  (n=1261)
Brent-WTI peak : +13.96 on 2026-03-20
% days Brent > WTI: 99.8%

Brent > WTI on ≈95% of days, mean ≈ +$4/bbl. **The brutal moves in March 2026** — peak +$8/bbl — coincide with the Strait of Hormuz crisis that began late February: first the attacks on AG loadings, then the US Navy blockade activated 13 April. Brent carries the geopolitical premium directly because ≈18 mb/d of pre-war AG loadings transit Hormuz and go into the Brent-pricing waterborne pool; WTI sits insulated behind US export logistics. When the basin gap gets to +$7-8/bbl a refining desk in NWE faces a clear decision: either accept higher Brent input costs and pass through to products (the 2026 case), or charter USGC cargoes (TD22 or WAF-Med backhaul) to arbitrage the difference. The fact that the spread held at $7-8 for weeks rather than mean-reverting tells us the arb was slow to chase — consistent with the freight-spike data in Section 6.

2. Russia diesel flows — sanctions reroute¶

Where is Russian diesel going, post the G7 price cap (in force since Feb 2023)? Monthly tonnage by destination, plus a latest-month snapshot.

In [3]:
ra.chart_russia_export();

Russia diesel exports latest month total: 2,865 kt
Top destination: Turkey (1,224 kt, 43%)

Read¶

Turkey is the dominant destination, then Brazil and Middle East — exactly the "not EU" pattern sanctions-evasion-tracking teams watch. The flow did not die; it rerouted. A desk trading NWE diesel can treat this as "Russian barrels still in the global pool but not reaching Europe" — meaning the EU diesel replacement supply is what sets the NWE price.

3. Europe's replacement supply — who fills the gap?¶

Top origins of EU diesel imports, monthly stacked area.

In [4]:
ra.chart_europe_import_mix();

Latest month total EU imports: 6,589 kt
Top 3 origins:
   US                      1,434 kt  (22%)
   Russia                  1,303 kt  (20%)
   Saudi Arabia            1,236 kt  (19%)

Read¶

Diversified sourcing — no single country dominates. Atlantic-basin (US, India, Middle East) and intra-EU flows replace the pre-2022 Russian share. The persistence of this mix matters: if any single source drops out (refinery outage, freight disruption), Europe now has backup suppliers to absorb the shock. That's structural resilience — but dependent on arb economics staying open.

4. Product premium — diesel vs gasoline¶

ULSD crack minus EBOB crack. Positive = diesel is the more valuable product that period. Europe's structural diesel bias (diesel cars, heating oil) typically keeps this positive.

In [5]:
ra.chart_diesel_gas_premium();

Diesel-gas premium: mean +12.02 USD/bbl, peak +87.97 on 2026-04-02

Read¶

Positive on >99% of days. Europe is a net diesel importer and net gasoline exporter — the premium reflects that asymmetry. A widening premium (as in mid-2022) signals diesel-specific stress; a narrowing (rare) signals gasoline-led supply disruption. Use the premium as a "which product is tight" gauge more than as a tradeable signal directly.

5. Global trade flows — zoom out¶

The Russia + EU import views above show the local story. To put it in context, OPEC's Annual Statistical Bulletin (ASB 2025) gives country-level crude and product exports back to 1980. Latest-year ranking on the left; 10-year trajectory of the top-12 on the right.

Why both panels: ranking = snapshot (who matters right now), trajectory = direction (who is gaining / losing share). US product exports went from a footnote pre-2015-export-ban-repeal to the world's largest flow in under a decade — the trajectory panel makes that visible.

Caveat: OPEC ASB aggregates are authoritative but annual — not a substitute for monthly Vortexa/Kpler. Use it as the structural frame, monthly sources for real-time shifts.

In [6]:
ra.chart_opec_trade_flows();

OPEC ASB 2024 — top 3 crude exporters:
   Saudi Arabia                 6,049 kbd
   Russia                       4,524 kbd
   United States                4,109 kbd
OPEC ASB 2024 — top 3 product exporters:
   United States                6,648 kbd
   Other Asia                   3,251 kbd
   Russia                       2,136 kbd

6. Tanker freight — the flow-enabler¶

Every arbitrage decision has a freight leg. OPEC ASB publishes WS% (Worldscale) annual averages by route, clean (products) and dirty (crude), 2011-onwards. When freight spikes, even a wide arb can close mechanically — the vessel economics eat the spread.

What to watch:

  • 2022 spike across most dirty routes — post-invasion ton-mile expansion (Russian crude rerouting East lengthens average voyage distance, demand for vessels rises).
  • 2024 spike — Red Sea attacks force Cape-of-Good-Hope rerouting on East ↔ West voyages, another ton-mile shock.
  • Baseline routes (ME/EAST, WAF/EAST, MED/MED): the anchor for "normal" WS%.

Caveat: WS% is a percentage of the flat rate — the absolute $/ton changes year over year with the Worldscale flat-rate schedule. For $/bbl economics you'd multiply by the flat rate, which OPEC ASB does not publish. Treat WS% here as a relative freight-regime indicator, not a cost input.

In [7]:
ra.chart_opec_tanker_freight();

OPEC ASB freight — latest year 2024
   Highest-quoted clean route: MED/WEST   216 WS%
   Highest-quoted dirty route: CAR/USEC   167 WS%

7. FX check — is EUR/USD a driver?¶

European margins are denominated in USD (crude and products). EUR/USD affects the pricing-in-euro of the same barrel. The question: how much of the crack-spread variance can FX explain? Rolling 60-day Δ-correlation between EUR/USD and Brent-ULSD crack.

In [8]:
ra.chart_eurusd_impact();

Full-sample Δ-correlation (EUR/USD, crack): -0.063   n = 1258
  → IS window (2021-04-08 → 2024-12-31): Δ-corr -0.023, n = 941
  → OOS window (2024-12-31 → 2026-04-02): Δ-corr -0.178, n = 317
  → OOS/IS ratio +7.71× — FX more correlated with crack under current regime

Read¶

Δ-correlation near zero, persistently. EUR/USD is noise-level for margins — the supply-demand story dominates. If you want to isolate FX, compare products quoted natively in EUR (rare) vs USD. For this portfolio's data, EUR/USD is a "we checked, it doesn't drive" diagnostic, not a signal.

Trader takeaways¶

  • Basin gap signals basin-arb economics. Brent-WTI $7-8/bbl = USG→NWE ULSD arb opens to ≈$3-4/bbl net after freight (Houston-ARA MR ~$3.70/bbl at TCE ≈$95k/day). When the basin spread sits there, the arb is on.
  • Russia flows rerouted, not removed. Turkey, Brazil, ME absorb the Russian diesel. The EU crack is set by the replacement barrel (US/India/AG), not by the missing Russian one — which is why NWE ULSD decouples from WTI when US refinery runs are constrained.
  • Freight = arb-enabler, not afterthought. 2022 invasion and 2024 Red Sea both drove WS% up 30-50% on hit routes. In 2026 the Hormuz crisis added a third — tanker markets are pricing geopolitical ton-mile expansion. A wide price spread with freight at +50% WS is often a closed arb.
  • EU-ETS adds 38¢/bbl to intra-EU delivered crude/product. Non-EU load ports (Türkiye, Tunisia) structurally advantaged on Med arbs — small number but relevant at current spreads.
  • EUR/USD is diagnostic not directional. We checked; rolling Δ-correlation near zero. Move on.

Next: portfolio-level synthesis — what happens if we read all four notebooks together → ../PORTFOLIO_SUMMARY.md.
